channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Stars of Israel’s TV Channel for Bibi Fans Turn on Trump
item.description: Leading figures from Channel 14, a right-wing broadcaster that supports Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, are openly attacking the U.S. president over his Iran deal.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Isabel Kershner/The New York Times
item.media:description: Yinon Magal, the host of “The Patriots,” Channel 14’s combative flagship talk show, at the studio in 2023.

item.title: Trump Angrily Defends Iran Deal, Belittling the One Obama Signed
item.description: President Trump denied that the United States would be part of a $300 billion rebuilding fund for Tehran and argued that his agreement was better than the one Barack Obama struck in 2015.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Allowing Iran to Charge Fees in Strait of Hormuz Would Set ‘Dangerous Precedent,’ Maersk CEO Says
item.description: With the United States and Iran nearing a deal to reopen the Persian Gulf, the mega shipping firm is treading cautiously.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Charlotte de la Fuente for The New York Times
item.media:description: “There’s been a couple of times where it was declared open and was not really open,” said Vincent Clerc, the chief executive of Maersk.

item.title: Mental Health Can Complicate Family Planning
item.description: Five stories show the hurdles that people with psychological diagnoses face when considering parenthood.
item.dc:creator: Christina Caron
item.media:credit: Bethany Mollenkof for The New York Times
item.media:description: Raising young twins is “really challenging,” Ms. Bui said. “But we’re doing well, considering.”

item.title: The U.S. Economy Is Leaving Small Businesses Behind
item.description: Small businesses say relentless pressures from tariffs and higher energy prices have sapped their resilience and finances.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Kaiti Sullivan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kirsten Davenport-Norwood, who owns Greene Thumb Landscape in Indianapolis

item.title: What to watch at the Fed’s meeting.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith

item.title: Warsh Faces Tenuous Balancing Act in First Fed Meeting as Chairman
item.description: Elevated inflation and the specter of rate increases will greet Kevin M. Warsh in his debut as chairman of the Federal Reserve.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chairman, has inherited a multitude of economic challenges.

item.title: Matthew Rhys, of ‘Widow’s Bay,’ Is on a Boat. Barely.
item.description: The star of this seafaring horror-comedy, which wraps up Season 1 on Wednesday, also spends his downtime on the water.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Blaise Cepis
item.media:credit: Blaise Cepis for The New York Times

item.title: Anthropic Employees Accuse Trump Administration of Targeting Them
item.description: Workers at the artificial intelligence company have been puzzled and increasingly concerned by the administration’s move to limit their latest A.I. models.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel, Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry/Bloomberg
item.media:description: Anthropic’s headquarters in San Francisco, where some employees are wondering if they are being picked on by President Trump.

item.title: Why Waymo’s Driverless Taxis Won’t Be on Your Streets Anytime Soon
item.description: The Silicon Valley company’s ambitions to roll out autonomous cars nationwide have hit political roadblocks in some of the country’s biggest markets.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo for The New York Times

item.title: The Cloud Has Sound: The Unrelenting and Unseen Cost of A.I. Data Centers
item.description: As tech giants rush to build infrastructure, some residents who live near data centers say a constant low-frequency vibration is ruining their health and homes.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Danielle Parhizkaran/The Boston Globe, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A 350,000-square-foot data center in Lowell, Mass., is abutted by homes. The site was once the Lowell Bleachery and Dye Works, and then for six decades the Prince Spaghetti factory.

item.title: Shane Hennen, the One Man Linking 3 Gambling Scandals, Is Ready to Talk
item.description: The N.B.A. and the N.C.A.A. have been rocked by indictments accusing players and others of fixing bets and playing in rigged poker games. Shane Hennen is named in each one.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar and Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Ryan Pfluger for The New York Times

item.title: This Senator Is an Internet Sensation. Is He Running for President?
item.description: The 39-year-old senator has become an internet sensation for Democrats seeking a 2028 contender. He says he’s focused on winning a second term in November.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jon Ossoff of Georgia is the only Senate Democrat seeking re-election in a state President Trump won in 2024.

item.title: Takeaways From the Runoff and Primary Elections in Georgia, Alabama and Oklahoma
item.description: Lt. Gov. Burt Jones lost the Republican runoff for governor to the health care executive Rick Jackson despite the president’s endorsement. Mr. Trump’s picks won in other races.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s chosen candidates won three Republican Senate primaries on Tuesday, but he but ran into trouble in other races in Georgia.

item.title: How Ecuador’s Andes Created a Language of Its Own
item.description: In Ecuador’s highlands, a seamless mix of Kichwa and Spanish creates a language that bends grammar, adds melody and goes unnoticed by many who speak it every day.
item.dc:creator: José María León Cabrera and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:credit: Johanna Alarcon for The New York Times
item.media:description: San Roque market in Quito, the capital of Ecuador, is one of the main meeting points between Kichwa and Spanish. These languages have long intertwined through exchanges in the city’s markets and public squares.

item.title: Elon Musk’s Feud With Delaware May Transform Corporate America
item.description: An angry Musk has taken his business elsewhere, and has urged other companies to follow suit.
item.dc:creator: Michael Steinberger

item.title: Gilgo Beach Serial Killer Receives Life Sentence After Guilty Plea
item.description: The decision on Wednesday will bring to an end an infamous case that took investigators more than a decade to solve.
item.dc:creator: Corey Kilgannon
item.media:credit: Pool photo by James Carbone
item.media:description: Rex Heuermann pleaded guilty to murdering eight women in the so-called Gilgo Beach killings at a court hearing in April.

item.title: Illinois Town Loses a Prison, Leaving Residents Worried About Jobs
item.description: Lincoln, Ill., where Abraham Lincoln once worked in the local courthouse, has lost two colleges, a bottle factory and now a state prison.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Buildings lining Lincoln’s downtown business district, which has gotten quieter over the years.

item.title: A Deadly Outbreak of Plague, Nearly 5,000 Years Before the Black Death
item.description: The oldest known cases, discovered among hunter-gatherers in Siberian graves, contradict the theory that the disease once was mild.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Intense Tornadoes Possible Across the Midwest on Wednesday
item.description: Forecasters warned that Illinois and Indiana faced the highest risk of severe storms throughout the day.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Tropical Storm Arthur Forms Near Texas, Bringing Heavy Rain and Flooding to the Gulf Coast
item.description: Arthur just became the first named storm of this year’s Atlantic hurricane season. It’s caused heavy rain and flooding in Texas this week, and more is on the way.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: The Real Reason Trump Never Stops Talking About Voter Fraud
item.description: “Voter fraud” is not about fraud. It is about who votes and how.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Tam Stockton for The New York Times

item.title: We Ran the Numbers. Remote Work Is Bad for Us.
item.description: Despite its advantages, work-from-home arrangements have significantly deepened Americans’ isolation and distress.
item.dc:creator: Emma Harrington and Natalia Emanuel
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times

item.title: What Could Happen in Iran?
item.description: Three Times reporters examine the possibilities.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Mangione to Use Emotional Disturbance as Defense in N.Y. Murder Trial
item.description: Luigi Mangione, who was accused of killing a health care executive in Manhattan in 2024, faces both state and federal charges.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Anusha Bayya
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Angelina Katsanis
item.media:description: Luigi Mangione in Manhattan Criminal Court on Wednesday.
